prompt: Hey! I'm hoping you can help me write something because I've rewritten this email four times and each version is worse than the last. I need to email my landlord asking to end my lease two months early because I got a job in another city. The lease technically has a penalty clause of one month's rent for early termination, but he's a reasonable guy and I've been a good tenant for three years, always paid on time, so I want to ask if he'd waive or reduce it, especially if I help him find a replacement tenant. Tone should be warm but not groveling, and it needs to be short enough that he actually reads it. Could you draft it for me?

Write a product description for an online store listing. Product: a stainless steel insulated water bottle, 750ml, keeps drinks cold 24 hours or hot 12 hours, leak-proof lid, fits car cup holders, available in 4 colors. Target audience: commuters and gym-goers. Length: 80-120 words, energetic but not shouty.

So my best friend is getting married next month and I somehow agreed to give a toast, which seemed like a fine idea at the time and now feels like a terrible one. Here's what you have to work with: we met in university when we were assigned as lab partners and she accidentally set our experiment on fire in week one. She's the most stubborn person I know, in a good way, she once drove six hours to return a wallet someone left at her cafe. Her partner Sam is the calm to her chaos and the only person who's ever out-stubborned her. I want it to be funny at the start, sincere at the end, about 90 seconds when spoken, and absolutely no clichés about wine getting better with age or whatever. Can you write a draft I can make my own?

Draft a polite but firm follow-up email. Context: I sent an invoice (#2041, 1,850) to a client 35 days ago, payment terms were 14 days, I already sent one gentle reminder 10 days ago and got no reply. This is the second reminder. I want to keep the relationship but make clear that a late fee applies after day 45 per our contract.

I run a tiny weekend coding club for kids at the local library and I need a short announcement for the community newsletter. The details, in no particular order because my notes are a mess: it's free, it runs every Saturday morning 10 to 12 starting September 6th, it's aimed at ages 10 to 14, no experience or own laptop needed since the library provides computers, parents can stay but don't have to, and spots are limited to 15 kids so people should email codingclub@example.org to register. Can you turn that pile of facts into something friendly and scannable, maybe 100 words, that doesn't sound like a legal notice? The newsletter audience is mostly parents and grandparents.

Rewrite this sentence to be clearer, then give two alternative versions with different tones (formal, casual): 'Due to the fact that the meeting was moved by management to a later point in time, the deliverables timeline will be impacted accordingly.'

I've been putting off writing my LinkedIn summary for about two years now and I've decided today is the day, with your help. Background: I'm a network operations engineer with 6 years of experience, mostly in banking infrastructure, I've led incident response for some fairly hairy outages, I'm certified in CCNP, and on the side I build small open-source tools, one of which has a few hundred users. What I want the summary to convey: reliable under pressure, genuinely enjoys the unglamorous work of keeping systems boring, and open to roles in site reliability. First person, around 120 words, confident without being insufferable — you know the LinkedIn tone I'm trying to avoid.

Write a 60-80 word app store description for a habit tracker app called Streakly. Key features: one-tap check-ins, streak freezes for sick days, private by default with no account required, home screen widget. Audience: people who have abandoned three other habit apps.

Outline a 5-part blog series about learning to cook for absolute beginners. For each part give a working title and 2-3 bullet points of content. The angle: cooking as a survival skill first, hobby second — aimed at people who currently order delivery five nights a week and feel vaguely guilty about it.
three subject line options for a newsletter about our cafe's new opening hours
